mnemonics.title: Chord Lookup Steps
mnemonics.content: FIND-SUCCESSOR: Follow fingers from largest to smallest that precede the ID. Each hop cuts remaining distance roughly in half.

mnemonics.title: Kademlia Routing
mnemonics.content: XOR Distance: Think of XOR (⊕) as 'similarity meter' - smaller distance = more similar IDs = closer in routing tree.

mnemonics.title: DHT Properties
mnemonics.content: DHT Benefits: SCALE-D (Scalable, Consistent hashing, Load balanced, Available, Decentralized, Fault tolerant)

acronyms.usage: Decentralized key-value storage system used in IPFS, BitTorrent, and blockchain networks for peer-to-peer data lookup.
Popular DHT protocol using XOR distance metric; used by BitTorrent, IPFS, and Ethereum's devp2p.
In Kademlia, each bucket stores up to k (typically 20) closest nodes to a prefix; oldest entries kept, newest evicted.
In Chord, routing table with m entries pointing to successors of exponentially increasing distances on the hash ring.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a circular clock face (hash ring). Nodes are placed around the clock at their hash positions. A finger table is like having 'jump pointers' to distant hours - e.g., from 3 o'clock you can jump to 4, 5, 7, 11, 3 (full circle). Key 7 finds its owner by walking clockwise until it hits a node.

Picture an upside-down binary tree. Root is empty prefix. Each branch splits by one bit (0 or 1). Your node ID determines your leaf position. You know nodes in subtrees at each depth - deeper = further in XOR distance. It's like knowing someone from every 'cousin' branch at different distances.

A sports track (oval). Keys are runners. Nodes are checkpoints. Each runner runs clockwise until hitting the first checkpoint, which 'owns' them. When a checkpoint (node) is added/removed, only nearby runners need to reassign - minimizing disruption.

analogies.analogy: DHT is like asking multiple friends to remember parts of your phonebook instead of keeping one master notebook. Any friend can help you find any number, and if one friend is unavailable, others still have the info.

Finger table is like having a phone with speed-dial to progressively distant contacts: best friend (1 hop away), cousin in same city, aunt in another state, friend in another country - each call can reach further across the network.

XOR distance is like measuring 'difference' between two random IDs. Similar IDs (small XOR) are like people with similar backgrounds - they share more 'neighbors' in common and can introduce you to similar groups of contacts.

Replication factor k is like having k photocopies of an important document stored in different offices. Even if one office burns down, you can get the document from others - but more copies = more storage cost.
